data.title: China ‘wheelchair anchor’ Liang Yi becomes first disabled TV host after spinal cord injury
data.description:
Chinese television personality Liang Yi became China’s first “wheelchair anchor” despite a sudden illness taking away her health and bright future.
Born in central China’s Hunan province in 1979, in 2001 Liang became an anchor at Hunan TV, one of China’s most popular television channels for the following two decades.

She had a bright future, but just four months after starting the job, she suddenly lost consciousness while backstage preparing for a show.
Liang was diagnosed with a spinal cord vascular malformation, which ruptured and compressed her central nervous system and paralysed her.
The rare condition is believed to be congenital.
The 22-year-old Liang recovered feeling in her upper limbs following surgery, but was paralysed from her chest down.

She said she initially felt anger and despair as she languished in hospital.
Liang’s feelings changed after she experienced strangers treating her kindly and becoming a Good Samaritan, cheering fellow patients up in hospital.
She also met renowned Chinese writer Shi Tiesheng, who was paralysed from the waist down at 21, but later moved many spirits with his optimistic and insightful writing.
Shi told her: “We are unaware of the despair we will encounter when we are young. The only thing we can do is read and think, so that we can fight the despair when we encounter it.”

Liang studied hard for three months and became a master’s student in broadcasting and hosting at China’s prestigious Communication University in 2011.
In 2012, she made a return to centre stage and became China’s first “wheelchair host” at Hunan TV.
Over the past decade, Liang hosted television and radio programmes, published books and attended activities to encourage and promote other people with disabilities.
Liang maintained a professional attitude to work despite her physical limitations.

She leaves home three hours in advance to prepare for recordings, even though her home is just 20 minutes away from work.
She also uses a cushion to keep her back straight during recordings. This makes her voice unstable, so she practices using the cushion at home to get used to it.
Liang said she is grateful to her mother, who is 11cm shorter than her but carried her to her wheelchair multiple times a day and accompanied her to work.
Liang said she made it to where she is today thanks to perseverance.
“Do not underestimate the power of persistence. We overcome difficulties and challenges in our life and become better people because of it,” she said.

data.title: China’s military eases physical requirements for pilots based on new cockpits
data.description:
China’s military has eased stringent physical requirements for pilots, based on the improved cockpits of the People’s Liberation Army’s newest warplanes.
Changes to height, weight and limb length requirements are among several revisions made to the physical selection criteria for pilots, military mouthpiece PLA Daily reported on Monday.
The report did not include the full revised set of criteria but said the changes had taken into account factors such as aircraft upgrades, advances in medical technology, and physical and mental changes in the population.
“Given changes to the position of the control stick and the increased adjustable angle of the seat in new fighter jets, the requirements for arm length and lower limb length have been appropriately lowered,” the report said.
China’s fifth-generation fighter jets including the J-20 and the J-35 have a sidestick configuration, as used by the F-16, F-35s and Dassault Rafale.


That configuration puts the control stick to the side – rather than at the centre – of the cockpit to improve ergonomics, since pilots no longer need to extend their arms to steer the centre yoke.
It also requires less physical effort – especially in high G-force situations – to steer the aircraft with a sidestick.
The sidestick layout is mostly used by modern fly-by-wire aircraft that use electronic signals rather than manual systems, eliminating the need for a large control column. Sidesticks also offer space efficiency and give the pilot a clearer view of the instrument panel.
Most of China’s new generation of fighter jets are equipped with this new layout.
Fourth-generation ejection seats for PLA warplanes have also been introduced that can be adjusted to suit pilots of various heights and weights, according to a report on state broadcaster CCTV in 2022. It said the system could also adjust the ejection force according to the pilot’s weight.
According to PLA Daily, the revised standards for pilots include the use of a 24-hour heart monitoring device, on top of routine electrocardiograms. It said ECGs were seen as “insufficient for a comprehensive assessment” of certain conditions.
One doctor who has screened pilot candidates for the Chinese military said testing was rigorous.
She said the most important tests were eyesight, the Bárány chair that tests balance and spatial orientation, and multitasking, where the candidate must operate a simple flight simulator while doing mathematical calculations.
“It’s really a pity seeing candidates passing these major hurdles but then failing to meet the stringent requirements for height, weight and limbs,” said the doctor, who spoke on condition of anonymity.
“Relaxing these requirements will definitely allow more candidates to join the ranks of pilots without compromising their combat performance,” she added.

The United States Air Force has struggled in recent years to recruit and retain pilots and has introduced policies including higher retention bonuses.
Meanwhile, China’s military has widened pilot recruitment in the past few years to meet the needs of its rapidly expanding aircraft carrier fleet and air force.
The PLA Navy loosened the selection criteria for pilots for its carrier-based aircraft and now allows candidates who have had vision correction surgery to apply.
In addition, the age limit for navy pilot cadets has been extended from 24 to 26 so that postgraduates can apply.

data.title: Hong Kong student delegation opts out of Japan exchange programme amid tensions
data.description:
A Hong Kong student delegation has decided to pull out of a regional exchange programme organised by Japan next month amid a recent diplomatic dispute between Beijing and Tokyo.
The Education Bureau confirmed on Saturday that Hong Kong would withdraw from this year’s Japan-East Asia Network of Exchange for Students and Youths Programme, citing safety concerns.
“In light of the recent increase in attacks on Chinese citizens in Japan and to ensure the safety of teachers and students, the Hong Kong delegation has, after careful consideration, withdrawn from this year’s [exchange programme],” the bureau said in a statement.
According to media reports, some 18 students and teachers from Hong Kong were supposed to fly to Japan for this year’s event, scheduled to start on December 7.
The Japanese government announced the programme in 2007 to promote people-to-people exchanges between Japan and various countries in the Asia-Pacific region, with the aim of establishing solidarity in Asia through youth exchange.
Hong Kong has been invited to join the programme since 2008.
A spokesman for Japan’s consulate in Hong Kong could not be reached for comment on Saturday afternoon.
The bureau’s decision also coincided with an escalating diplomatic spat between China and Japan over recent comments by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi on defending Taiwan.
In an apparent rebuttal by Beijing, the foreign ministry urged Chinese people to reconsider travel to Japan, citing “safety risks” amid a rise in cases of attacks against Chinese people.
The Hong Kong government also followed suit and updated its travel alert to advise residents to exercise caution and prioritise personal safety while in Japan.
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific also said it would allow travellers holding tickets to Japan to “modify” their travel plans and promised to “do our utmost to provide flexibility and accommodate their needs”.
Cathay’s budget arm, HK Express, also made similar arrangements.
Greater Bay Airlines revealed it would offer refunds for Japan-bound flights, applicable to tickets bought on or before November 15 and scheduled to fly on or before December 31.
Hong Kong Airlines said on Friday that it was committed to helping affected passengers with their travel arrangements. Passengers who booked their flights to Japan directly can contact its customer service team or travel agent for help.
About 491,000 air tickets from mainland China to Japan were cancelled within a short period after carriers swiftly announced refunds or itinerary changes for flights booked through December 31, according to a veteran aviation analyst.
Japan’s foreign affairs ministry, however, rejected that there had been a surge in criminal cases against Chinese nationals in the country, saying “such an assertion is incorrect”.
It cited national police agency figures that the number of murder, robbery and arson cases involving Chinese people as victims in Japan had dropped from 48 in 2023 to 45 in 2024.
As of end-October this year, there were 28 such cases, a drop from 35 for the same period in 2024.

data.title: Interactive museum dedicated to the Shroud of Turin opens in Southern California
data.description:
An interactive museum dedicated to the Shroud of Turin, which some Christians claim was Jesus’ burial cloth, opens its doors to the public this week at the Christ Cathedral campus in Southern California.
“The Shroud of Turin: An Immersive Experience”, a US$5 million exhibition in Garden Grove featuring 360-degree projection rooms, Shroud of Turin replicas, interactive kiosks, and a life-size sculpture of Christ, was conceived over three years and funded through private donations.
The content was created primarily by Othonia, a Rome-based group dedicated to the examination of the shroud. The original – a cloth 4.4 metres (14 feet 5 inches) long and 1.1 metres wide – is kept in a bulletproof, climate-controlled case housed in the Cathedral of Saint John the Baptist in Turin, Italy.
The California museum – covering 930 square metres (10,000 sq ft) – contains a life-size laminated visual of the shroud stretched over a wall. It shows the faint image of a man with wounds similar to Christ’s in the story.
The Vatican, while making no claim to the original’s authenticity, has called the cloth a powerful symbol of Christ’s suffering. In 1988, carbon-dating of scraps of the cloth put it as being made in the 13th or 14th century, but some say the results might have been skewed by contamination, calling for larger samples to be analysed.
Many insist that the cloth contains pollen from Jerusalem and is woven in a pattern that was unique to the first century.

The cloth, considered a relic by many Christians and which may never leave Turin, was most recently on display in the spring of 2015.
The new exhibition will remain at least until 2030 on the Christ Cathedral campus, next to the glass tower and sanctuary erected by televangelist Robert H Schuller that is now home to the Catholic Diocese of Orange.
Timothy Freyer, an auxiliary bishop at the diocese, has high hopes that the exhibition will reinforce for visitors the power of God’s love for all.
“My hope is that people of faith will have stronger faith, people who are doubting will come to belief, and people with no faith will begin the questioning process and then come to faith,” he said.
One of the highlights of the immersive experience is a recreation of the story of Christ’s resurrection on Easter morning, with museum visitors sitting in the tomb and watching the shrouded body disappear in a flash of light. The exhibition also features replicas of the spear believed to have pierced Christ’s chest and the helmet of thorns placed on his head.
Philip Rizzo, a parishioner at St Bonaventure Catholic Church in Huntington Beach who was part of a group that got a preview, said seeing these items up close created a powerful visual representation of Christ’s suffering and sacrifice.
“We’re tangible beings, not just spirit and light,” Rizzo said. “Science really helps make that connection.”
While some scientists have criticised the eagerness of Christians to authenticate the cloth, the new exhibition had its origins in such curiosity. Its main proponent was August Accetta, a gynaecologist who opened up the Shroud Centre of Southern California in 1998 and has studied the cloth for over three decades.
Accetta, who grew up a Catholic, said it was the Shroud of Turin that converted him from agnostic back to Catholicism. His fascination continued after meeting John Jackson, a nuclear physicist, who in 1978 led a 40-person team of scientists as part of the Shroud of Turin Research Project. Their study determined that the cloth was not a manufactured work of art, but contained human bloodstains and that no known physical, chemical or biological process could adequately explain how the image on the Shroud was formed.
Accetta believes the image was created by a burst of radiation and that a massive amount of energy would have been required to create the image without burning the cloth.
He believes the body of Christ became light and the shroud simply collapsed, leaving the imprint of his face on the white linen.

Robert Spitzer, founder of the Magis Centre, a non-profit that uses science to defend the Catholic faith, said the cloth bears “perfect bloodstains down to the edges”, which does not happen when a shroud is simply pulled off a dead body. The stains should be smeared and fragmented if that were the case, he said. Like Accetta, Spitzer believes the etching on the cloth was likely caused by particle radiation.
Spitzer added that he does not need a relic to explain his faith because that comes from his belief in scripture and Christ’s resurrection.
“But it’s given me a little deeper insight into God and how he works,” he said.
Nora Creech, director of Othonia in the US, said the Rome-based organisation was founded by Hector Guerra, a Catholic priest who dreamed of creating 100 exhibitions globally. He built the first flagship exhibition in Jerusalem and followed with others in Rome, Poland, Mexico and the US. This latest one is the first to feature an immersive experience, which was created by a California-based studio.
Creech said they decided to begin the movie-like immersive experience with Christ’s life story from birth to crucifixion and end with the resurrection and the message that “Jesus is still with us today”.
“One of the quotes in the movie is that the cross received Jesus alive and turned him over to the shroud, dead. The shroud received Jesus dead and turned him over to us alive in the Eucharist,” she said.
Rudy Dichtl, a Catholic and one of the scientists who formed the 1978 research team, still remembers every moment spent touching and holding the cloth in his hands.
Dichtl said as a scientist he acknowledges there is limited evidence in science to conclusively say the cloth was the one that covered the body of Christ.
“But as a Christian,” he said, “I do believe it is the burial cloth of Christ.”

data.title: How Cop30 highlights the power of Chinese state capitalism
data.description:
It is not quite true to say that China stole the show with its strong presence at the recent Cop30 climate summit in Belem, Brazil, because the United States had already voluntarily abdicated its lead role in these events. However, the summit did underline the staying power of state versus market economies when it comes to long-term investment.
The truth is that Western approaches to climate change prevention or alleviation have been half-hearted and muddled from the start. They have relied largely on uncoordinated private-sector initiatives while China has adopted a more coordinated and effective approach.
There is a strong analogy here with the provision of physical infrastructure – which includes transport, energy and communications – where China’s gleaming new facilities contrast with sometimes rusting, outdated and overburdened structures in market economies. Both situations offer lessons in comparative capitalism.
The reason is that when it comes to the need for huge and long-term capital investments with uncertain returns financially, though not socially, market economies tend to fall short. The free market simply does not deliver the goods at the scale required.
The shortcomings of relying on the free market to channel resources where they are most needed became even clearer during the UN climate conference in Brazil. Reuters led its November 15 report from Belem with this: “With the United States absent from the UN annual international climate summit for the first time in three decades, China is stepping into the limelight as a leader in the fight against global warming.”
The same report also quoted California Governor Gavin Newsom as saying, “China gets it. America is toast competitively if we don’t wake up to what the hell they’re doing in this space, on supply chains, how they’re dominating manufacturing, how they’re flooding the zone.”

It is not just that US President Donald Trump rejects the notion of climate change – he called it “the greatest con job ever perpetrated on the world” during the UN General Assembly in September – or that Bill Gates has urged the world to stop focusing on preventing climate change and shift to dealing with its impact instead. The fact is that the US has not been able to show it can afford to foot the bill for dealing with, preventing or adapting to climate change, or that it has the right mechanisms for dealing with the problem. Instead, it tries to deny climate change and how China appears to have found a better approach.
Governments across the globe have tried to kick the can ever further down the road on climate change. Ever since then-UN Secretary General Kofi Annan espoused the notion in 2004 that all would be well if only big companies could be persuaded to follow environmental, social and governance (ESG) rules, it has been left to the free market to ensure compliance.
That has not had the desired effect. As a result, the carbon dioxide emissions that help drive climate change have continued to rise to the point that it is almost certain that humanity has missed its chance to limit global warming to a 1.5 degrees Celsius increase. In the meantime, temperatures and climate change-driven natural disasters continue to increase.


Likewise, the assumption in market economies that financial markets would or could foot the massive cost of financing solutions to the climate problem has proved to be unfounded. Former Bank of England governor Mark Carney’s claim that private financial institutions were committed to providing more than US$130 trillion in capital to reach net zero emissions has turned out to be little more than hot air.
In contrast, China has pushed ahead with expanding green finance while other countries focus on their own economies and arming their militaries. A May report by Climate and Capital Media noted that “China’s approach to the green finance model could make it a global leader, but in many ways it is already becoming a leader for Global South countries, especially in Asia.” The report also highlighted China’s debut of its first green sovereign bond on the London Stock Exchange in April.
There is justifiable criticism of China for its heavy dependence on carbon-emitting coal to fuel its power stations, but it has also become a major force in global renewable energy investment and finance. This is providing not only China but also much of Asia and beyond with the financing needed to shift from brown to green energy.


Key to this is the willingness of China’s central and local governments and state entities to issue or guarantee green bonds on a scale that is unmatched elsewhere. No amount of ESG promotion or the launch of climate funds on Western markets – and far less their reliance upon individual company efforts – can match this.
Things are slowly changing. For example, the Group of Boutique Asset Managers, a global network of senior executives from independent asset management firms, has identified a decisive shift in global investors’ focus, away from ESG investing and towards broader “resilience-led investing”, with a longer-term perspective in mind.
But what is pivotal in the costly battle against climate change is coordination at the state level of financing initiatives, whether through sovereign green bonds or government guarantees of such bonds issued by quasi-government entities. It is time to study the Chinese model in greater depth.

data.title: Ann Leda Shapiro, whose feminist art was once censored, holds Hong Kong exhibition
data.description:
American artist Ann Leda Shapiro was right in the thick of it when art, war, feminism and the Aids epidemic collided in the 20th century.
Born in 1946, she created gender-questioning art in the early 1970s that the Whitney Museum of American Art censored, protested for peace alongside renowned poet Allen Ginsberg, and volunteered at a traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) acupuncture clinic that treated Aids patients.
A few years ago, Shapiro revealed that she was a member of the feminist artist collective Guerrilla Girls in the 1980s, and that she came up with the iconic gorilla masks that protected the anonymity of members after she once accidentally misspelt the group’s name as “Gorilla”.
Recently, the 79-year-old former beatnik and hippie was at Hong Kong’s Axel Vervoordt Gallery for the opening of her first solo exhibition in Asia, featuring a new watercolour series titled “Body is Landscape”.
“I grew up when we thought we could change the world, where people helped each other and kindness was natural,” she tells the Post, reflecting on the current political landscape.

This is why she continues to infuse her art with ideas from her TCM healing practice and her political beliefs.
Her watercolours weep for the tragedy of atomic warfare and the Fukushima nuclear accident, while incorporating the TCM dualities of brightness and darkness to reflect the dynamic nature of the world.
“I’m interested in the idea of ‘matter on the verge of becoming’,” she says. Her paintings, whether purely landscape or including human figures, aim to depict “something that’s living and dying at the same time”.
The forms in her watercolours, often outlined with pencil, are organic and dynamic – seemingly in a state of constant change.
Shapiro even treats her own works as living beings. For Radiating Growth (2022), she pasted a previously finished painting onto a larger piece of paper and reworked it into a bigger one because it “needed room to breathe”.

She was inspired by American painter Charles E. Burchfield, who similarly expanded pieces decades after their original creation. For Shapiro, this technique exposes the process of creation and documents the artist’s natural growth and maturity.
Her life has been both extraordinary and ordinary, she says. Before her art was accepted by renowned art institutions like the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in New York, Shapiro was just an eccentric kid from the Big Apple.
She grew up next door to the American Museum of Natural History and across the park from the Metropolitan Museum of Art, during a time when post-war art movements were exploding in New York.
Her parents were communists who raised her as a “red diaper baby”, and her mother would take her to weekend classes at MoMA as a child.
After saving enough money to study at the San Francisco Art Institute in 1965, Shapiro befriended Ginsberg, and they marched against the Vietnam war together.
“Allen peed for peace, too. He would stop and pee on every church,” she recalled, laughing.
Her boyfriend at the time was the drummer of American psychedelic rock band Big Brother and the Holding Company, which led her to live with legendary musicians, including rock singer-songwriter Janis Joplin.

Shapiro remembers her time at the University of California, Davis, where she worked towards a master’s degree in fine arts, with dejection. She burst into tears when her all-male graduate committee challenged her work, asking if she was “purposely trying to be primitive”.
It is therefore unsurprising that Shapiro’s art challenges gender stereotypes.
Shapiro later realised that her paintings, influenced by Mexican folk art at the time, also explore ideas consistent with the philosophies behind TCM – something she later learned in the 1980s from volunteering at an acupuncture clinic for Aids patients after a close friend contracted the disease. After receiving formal training, she ran her own practice from 1991 to 2020.
“The body is a landscape that is connected to both the female and male, yin and yang, which are in constant motion,” she explains. According to TCM, qi – the body’s vital energy source – depends on harmony between the two energies to avoid sickness and disease.
This duality formed her radical paintings, such as Two Sides of Self (1971), which depict hermaphroditic mermaids, and Woman Landing on Man in the Moon or One Needs a Cock to Get By (1971), portraying an astronaut with visible breasts and a penis.

Initially ecstatic after meeting curators from the Whitney Museum of American Art to organise a solo show of her work, Shapiro was shocked when they censored those two paintings.
“I wasn’t savvy enough to get a lawyer … and then later I saw other [artists in censorship battles] sue and get major careers – they were men.”
She was eventually vindicated when the Seattle Art Museum acquired the two paintings 40 years later, but this upsetting incident shifted her focus elsewhere: academia.
She taught visual arts at San Francisco State University, specialising in criticism with a feminist approach. Then, often the only woman in the art department, she spent the next couple of decades relocating from state to state while also taking part in overseas programmes from Africa to Fiji.
She now lives in a small island community off the coast of Washington state. Her visual motif of trees, particularly nurse logs – fallen trees that provide water and nutrients to seedlings or young trees growing on top of them – emphasises communal interdependence and mutual aid.

Painting is a “meditative experience” in what Shapiro describes as today’s “age of anxiety”. While many assume she dots the recurring white circles in her compositions in an acupuncture-like manner, she actually paints around the individual circles in a ritualistic manner.
The era of beatniks and hippies may have gone, but Shapiro continues to build on her legacy by painting from her island home, practising acupuncture within her community and sharing her work with international audiences.
For her first solo exhibition in Asia, she hopes that “people could come and appreciate the work, and maybe think about the world in a more expansive way”.
“Ann Leda Shapiro: Body is Landscape”, Axel Vervoordt Gallery, 21/F, Coda Designer Centre, 62 Wong Chuk Hang Road, Wong Chuk Hang, Tuesday-Saturday, 11am-7pm. Until March 7.

data.title: Labrador lost on China beach found 1,500km away 3 months later, reunited with owner
data.description:
A pet dog in China has been miraculously found 1,500km from home and reunited with her owner three months after she went missing.
September the Labrador was lost on August 13, at a beach in Qingdao, in eastern China’s Shandong province.
According to her owner, surnamed Gao, the dog walked out by herself while playing in her garden. By the time she noticed, September was already missing.

She checked the surveillance footage from a nearby beer festival and discovered September walking away with another dog and its owner.
Gao never gave up the search for her pet.
She toured animal shelters in the city and kept looking for online posts that might help her find her beloved pet.
Gao also travelled to other cities whenever she saw dogs that looked like hers.
On November 6, Gao found videos of a Labrador posted online by a woman in Changsha, 1,500km away from her city in central China’s Hunan province.

She immediately recognised September because of the marks on her body caused by a skin condition.
In a video call with the woman, surnamed Zhou, Gao called September’s name and saw the dog reacting to her.
“I immediately cried with joy,” Gao recalled.
Zhou discovered September wandering in the street in heavy rain at 3am on November 4.

Worried that she might get ill, she took the dog to her dormitory at work, bought food and prepared a blanket for it.
When Zhou posted videos of September in the hope of finding her owner, some people said the animal had been wandering around the neighbourhood for days.
Gao said she dared not imagine how September managed to travel so far.
“She might have been stolen or taken there by tourists to Qingdao. Whatever the case, she had a rough time out there.”
After making several more videos to confirm it was her dog, Gao called a pet relocation service and spent 1,100 yuan (US$155) to bring September back on November 8.
“I do not want to waste one more second,” she said.
Gao said September jumped into her arms the moment she saw her and even cried.

She said her dog was healthy and had even successfully lost some weight.
The animal’s temperament had also changed a little. She was less lazy and more willing to help them fetch things like slippers.
Many were touched by Gao’s unwavering search for September.
“It is a miracle that you found her after she was lost for such a long time and strayed so far,” said one online observer.
“‘Lost and found is one of the most beautiful things in the world,” another said.
Gao said she would microchip September so that she will never lose her again.

data.title: Xiaomi open-sources AI model spanning autonomous driving and robotics
data.description:
Xiaomi has open-sourced a new foundation model that integrates autonomous driving and embodied artificial intelligence, as the Chinese smartphone and electric-vehicle (EV) manufacturer seeks to enhance its AI capabilities.
MiMo-Embodied, which comes after Xiaomi’s first large language model MiMo launched in April, was described as the “first open-source vision-language model” that combined autonomous driving and embodied AI tasks. Technical reports and project files are available on platforms such as Hugging Face and GitHub.
The cross-embodied model achieved state-of-the-art performance in both domains, according to Xiaomi It demonstrated “superior” results in task planning, affordance prediction and spatial understanding for embodied AI, while excelling in environmental perception, status prediction and drive planning for autonomous driving.
Interest in embodied intelligence, which integrates AI into physical systems, has surged alongside rapid advancements in humanoid robotics in China.
Xiaomi debuted prototypes of a quadrupedal robot dog and a humanoid robot in 2021 and 2022, respectively, and has been heavily investing in related areas.


Xiaomi’s announcement on Friday followed the recent hiring of Luo Fuli, a former core researcher at Chinese AI start-up DeepSeek who joined Xiaomi several months after the company reportedly approached her.
Luo, who is part of the MiMo-Embodied project, wrote on WeChat earlier this month that she was working with “a team of creative, talented and dedicated researchers” to build a future where “intelligence will step beyond language into the physical world”.
Luo is scheduled to speak at Xiaomi’s “Human × Car × Home” partner conference in December, where she will present as head of the MiMo team.
As a latecomer to China’s automotive market, Xiaomi has rapidly scaled production. This week, the company said its 500,000th electric vehicle had rolled off the line at its Beijing plant, just 19 months after its first delivery.
This week, Xiaomi reported an over 22 per cent increase in third-quarter revenue to 113.1 billion yuan (US$15.9 billion), with adjusted net profit rising 81 per cent to 11.3 billion yuan.
EVs, AI initiatives and other new businesses generated their first profit, with revenue reaching 29 billion yuan – almost triple that of a year earlier. The surge came despite heightened scrutiny over the safety of Xiaomi vehicles following two fatal accidents involving its self-driving system.
Founder and CEO Lei Jun has issued several public statements emphasising safety and regulatory compliance.
Xiaomi’s Hong Kong-listed shares closed at HK$38 on Friday, translating to a market capitalisation of about HK$992 billion.

data.title: Hong Kong to ‘radiate’ mainland China’s strengths, tech to aid Belt and Road nations
data.description:
Hong Kong’s chief executive has pledged to harness the city’s unique advantages to bring mainland China’s strengths, technology and compassion to the Belt and Road nations while fostering people-to-people ties at the launch of a charity’s headquarters founded by a former city leader.
“Hong Kong has been leveraging its ‘hard power’ in professional services and ‘soft power’ in social cohesion to foster people-to-people bonds in Belt and Road countries,” Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said at the opening of the GX Foundation’s global headquarters in Sai Ying Pun on Saturday.
Lee touted the foundation’s efforts, led by former chief executive Leung Chun-ying, to provide humanitarian medical and public health aid and to reduce the incidence of infectious diseases across multiple nations.
The foundation was established by Leung in 2018 and focuses on humanitarian aid in countries under the Belt and Road Initiative, such as treating cataract blindness, combating dengue fever and improving water safety. Its work has already extended across 10 countries, including Honduras, Fiji, Laos, Cambodia and Djibouti.
Lee added that the foundation had been actively promoting transnational exchanges and cooperation by offering training and internships in humanitarian aid to young people.
“[The Foundation] nurtures the humanitarian spirit that embraces the world and inspires future generations to be a new force in foreign aid, demonstrating China’s commitment to building a community with a shared future,” Lee said.
“Hongkongers are proficient in both Chinese and English and possess extensive networks spanning the mainland and the world.
“Hong Kong will continue to leverage its radiating and driving force to bring China’s strengths, technology and compassion to Belt and Road countries, fostering people-to-people bonds.”
The ceremony was also attended by Zhou Ji, the director of Beijing’s Liaison Office in Hong Kong, and Li Yong-sheng, the acting commissioner of the Chinese Foreign Ministry’s office in Hong Kong.

Li commended the foundation for its proactive response to Chinese President Xi Jinping’s call to cement people-to-people bonds between China and the world.
“[The GX Foundation] fully leveraged Hong Kong’s institutional strength under ‘one country, two systems’, and its unique advantage of enjoying backing from the motherland and extensive connections to the world,” Li said, pointing to the foundation’s projects in carrying out medical and health aid abroad.
Li also pledged to support the foundation to the best of his office’s ability in reaching out to more Belt and Road countries.
Leung said his foundation had been promoting international people-to-people exchanges and deepening international connections and cooperation, echoing the central government’s demand for Hong Kong.
“We built a credible, affectionate and respectable image of the Chinese people worldwide, and we plan to do even more in the future,” he said, adding that the opening of the headquarters marked a new chapter for the organisation.
The new global headquarters of the foundation, situated in a five-storey main building with a single-storey annexe at No 4 Hospital Road, Sai Ying Pun, was built in 1921 in response to a plague outbreak and served as a residence for cleaners and later as a shelter for street sleepers.
The foundation was granted the site by the government at a nominal premium of HK$1,000 (US$128) under a private treaty grant in July this year.

data.title: Bangladesh seeks to fix its ‘completely broken’ politics with a referendum for change
data.description:
With ex-leader Sheikh Hasina sentenced to death in absentia for crimes against humanity, Bangladesh is turning its focus towards a referendum aimed at reforming its political system and rebuilding its democratic institutions.
Interim leader Muhammad Yunus, the Nobel laureate who assumed office after last year’s uprising, earlier this month pledged to hold a national vote on the blueprint for change, timed to coincide with February’s planned parliamentary election.
But analysts have cast doubt on whether the “July Charter” at the centre of the proposed reforms – named for the month that Hasina’s regime was toppled – can be successfully implemented in the South Asian nation of 174 million.
Yunus, 85, previously said he inherited a “completely broken” political system. He has championed the charter as essential to prevent a relapse into the authoritarianism that marked Hasina’s years-long rule.
“It’s the birth of a new Bangladesh,” he said at a signing ceremony for the new charter on October 17.

But the main obstacle to change is Bangladesh’s lack of “democratic culture”, rather than a deficiency of legal frameworks, according to Md. Rizwanul Islam, dean of the School of Humanities and Social Sciences at the North South University in Dhaka.
“We have witnessed a ‘winner-takes-all syndrome’ in Bangladesh again and again,” he told This Week in Asia. “Elected governments have turned into authoritarian regimes”.
Without substantive change, Islam said the country risked repeating this drift into authoritarianism. “Unfortunately, I cannot see any unequivocal sign of a seismic shift in our political culture to engender people-oriented reform in the near future.”
Checks and balances
The July Charter seeks to create checks and balances, including introducing two-term limits for prime ministers, expanding the powers for the presidency and creating explicit recognition of Bangladesh’s multi-ethnic, multi-religious character.
Voters will be asked to respond to a single, four-part referendum question; if approved, a newly formed Constitutional Reform Council would be tasked with translating this mandate into law through amendments passed by parliament.
The country’s major political parties, including Hasina’s Awami League and the Bangladesh Nationalist Party, have long resisted meaningful internal reform and power-sharing, preferring centralised decision-making instead.


Yunus’ proposed reforms “look good on paper” but much would depend on their implementation, said Michael Kugelman, a senior fellow at the Asia-Pacific Foundation of Canada who specialises in South Asian politics.
“These are major political changes and there will be vested interests that will likely try to resist, even if the reforms enjoy a mandate from the people,” he said. “If the referendum passes, will the next government actually follow through?”
Though the post-uprising reform agenda had initially enjoyed broad support, Kugelman said “the public soured on it” amid mounting economic woes, law-and-order challenges and a lack of transparency.
“The referendum will make clear whether or not people are setting aside their scepticism to support measures that had been widely backed soon after Hasina’s ousting,” he said.
Hasina’s early political career was defined by her opposition to military rule, but her second stint as Bangladesh’s prime minister – lasting 15 years to 2024 – was increasingly marked by accusations of authoritarianism, corruption, suppression of dissent and election manipulation, which led to widespread protests and her eventual ousting.
On Monday, a Bangladeshi tribunal sentenced her to death for ordering last year’s crackdown on protests that left as many as 1,400 dead. It also found her guilty of incitement and failing to prevent atrocities. Hasina, self-exiled in India, has rejected the ruling.
‘Hijacked’ reforms
The July Charter aimed to “prevent the tyranny of the majority” through constitutional amendments that would avert the kind of unchecked rule “we have seen in the past”, said Lailufar Yasmin, an international relations professor at the University of Dhaka.
“Having an absolute majority after winning an election does not automatically give a political party the power to govern as it wishes,” she told This Week in Asia.
But Lailufar said she was sceptical that Bangladesh’s political parties were ready to “relinquish their tendency to engage in narrow power struggles in favour of establishing a system of checks and balances that promotes accountability”.
In a functioning democracy, electoral politics should be about “coexistence and the well-being of people, rather than merely a tool for capturing power”, she said.

Lailufar worries that the reform drive has been “hijacked” by party politics, which “may lead people to vote along party lines on the reform proposals, without understanding the essence of the proposals.”
While most parties, including the Bangladesh Nationalist Party and Bangladesh Jamaat-e-Islami, backed the charter during last month’s signing ceremony, the National Citizen Party – formed by the leaders of last year’s uprising – refused, as did four left-leaning parties, citing a lack of binding legal guarantees.
Critics also argue that some of the nation’s founding principles, such as secularism, were watered down or omitted from the charter during the drafting process.
